questions.question: Explain the purpose of try, catch, and throw keywords in C++ exception handling.
keyPoints: try block contains code that might throw an exception
catch block handles the thrown exception
throw keyword creates and throws an exception object
try-catch separates error detection from error handling

questions.question: What is stack unwinding in C++? Why is it important?
keyPoints: Stack unwinding is destroying local objects as exceptions propagate
Happens when exception moves up the call stack
Ensures proper cleanup of resources (RAII)
Prevents memory leaks from unreleased resources

questions.question: What is the difference between catch(Type e) and catch(...)?
keyPoints: catch(Type e) catches specific exception type and provides access to exception object
catch(...) catches all exception types regardless of content
catch(...) cannot access the exception object
catch(...) acts as universal handler

questions.question: What is the purpose of the noexcept specifier in C++?
keyPoints: noexcept indicates a function will not throw exceptions
Allows compiler optimizations
If noexcept function throws, std::terminate() is called
Used for functions that guarantee no exceptions

questions.question: Explain the standard exception hierarchy in C++.
keyPoints: std::exception is the base class for all standard exceptions
std::runtime_error and std::logic_error are important derived classes
std::bad_alloc for memory allocation failures
std::out_of_range for index errors

questions.question: How do you rethrow an exception in C++? Explain with an example.
keyPoints: Use throw; (without object) to rethrow current exception
Useful for logging before passing exception to outer handler
Only valid inside a catch block
Can add additional information when rethrowing

questions.question: What happens if no catch block matches a thrown exception?
keyPoints: Program calls std::terminate() function
std::terminate() calls std::abort() by default
Program terminates abnormally
Prevents undefined behavior from uncaught exceptions

questions.question: Write a C++ program demonstrating exception handling with multiple catch blocks for different exception types.
keyPoints: Include try block with code throwing different types
Include catch blocks for std::exception, std::runtime_error, std::out_of_range
Include catch(...) as universal handler
Demonstrate catch block order matters
Show proper exception handling flow

questions.question: Create a custom exception class inheriting from std::exception and demonstrate its use in a C++ program.
keyPoints: Define custom exception class inheriting from std::exception
Override what() virtual method
Throw custom exception in appropriate scenario
Catch using base class std::exception pointer
Demonstrate polymorphic exception handling

questions.question: Explain exception safety guarantees in C++ with examples.
keyPoints: Basic guarantee: valid state, no leaks but may change
Strong guarantee: either succeeds or no change (transaction semantics)
Nothrow guarantee: never throws, always succeeds
Use RAII and smart pointers for exception safety

questions.question: Write a C++ program that demonstrates stack unwinding when an exception is thrown from a nested function call.
keyPoints: Create nested function calls with local objects
Throw exception from innermost function
Show destructor calls during unwinding
Demonstrate proper cleanup of resources
Explain importance for resource management
